title: Rum and raisin fudge
subtitle: Soft Scottish-style fudge with rum-soaked raisins
excerpt: Rum and raisin fudge is a condensed milk fudge cooked to soft-ball stage then beaten until thick and grainy. The raisins are soaked in dark rum before folding in at the end. Scottish-style fudge is softer and more crumbly than tablet and does not require long beating. It sets in about two hours and cuts into clean squares.
sourceNotes: Condensed milk fudge is a British and Scottish confectionery tradition, with Scottish tablet being the firmer, more crystalline relation. The rum and raisin flavour combination in fudge is documented in British confectionery from the mid-twentieth century. The soft-ball stage cooking method gives the creamy, slightly crumbly texture typical of commercial Scottish-style fudge. This recipe is synthesised from the established British fudge tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap individual squares in greaseproof paper and freeze in a single layer. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for two weeks in an airtight container at room temperature.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no baking required; fudge is cooked on the hob to soft-ball stage at 116 degrees C

glossaryTerms.definition: Soft-ball stage is 115 to 118 degrees C. At this temperature a small drop of the syrup dropped into cold water forms a soft, pliable ball that flattens when pressed. This is the target for fudge. Below this temperature the fudge will be too soft to cut; above it the fudge will be hard and crystalline. Use a sugar thermometer for accuracy. If you do not have one, test the soft-ball stage by dropping a small amount of the mixture into a cup of cold water: it should form a ball you can squash between your fingers.

content.text: The fudge mixture reaches 116 degrees C. Keep children away from the hob throughout. Do not leave the pan unattended once the mixture is boiling.
is the critical step. Undercooked fudge is too soft; overcooked fudge is grainy and hard. Use a sugar thermometer. Once you hit 116 degrees C, pull the pan off the heat immediately.
Soak the raisins and prepare the tin
Put the 100 g raisins in a small bowl, pour over the 3 tbsp rum and leave to soak for at least 30 minutes. Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper.

Put the 397 g condensed milk, 350 g light brown sugar, 100 g butter, 100 ml milk and half a teaspoon salt into a medium saucepan. Stir over low heat until the sugar dissolves completely. Increase to medium heat and bring to the boil, stirring frequently. Cook, stirring every minute or so, until the mixture reaches 116 degrees C on a sugar thermometer, about 15 to 20 minutes.

Remove from the heat. Leave to cool for 5 minutes without stirring. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon or electric hand mixer for 5 to 8 minutes until the fudge thickens, loses its gloss, and starts to feel grainy. Drain the raisins and fold them in quickly. Pour into the prepared tin and smooth the top. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours until firm. Cut into 3 cm squares.

items.problem: Fudge is too soft to cut after 2 hours in the fridge
items.solution: The mixture was not cooked to 116 degrees C. Refrigerate for another 2 hours; if still soft, it will not set firm enough to cut cleanly. The next batch should be cooked until a thermometer clearly reads 116 degrees C.

items.problem: Fudge is hard, dry and crumbly
items.solution: The temperature went above 120 degrees C, pushing it toward tablet territory. Pull the pan from the heat the moment the thermometer reads 116 degrees C.
